description: A physical thing or material is 'hiso' when it is free of the unwanted matter or residue relevant to its present use. A bowl can be clean enough for a meal whatever the purity of its material. 'hureki' (dirty) points to unwanted matter that is present; clean makes the wider practical judgement about what remains.
articulatory_notes: Let unvoiced air pass through the open glottis for 'h' and continue into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Raise the tongue blade towards the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 's', keep the narrow airflow moving, and round the lips into true-mid back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: Unvoiced air continues through both consonants, first openly at 'h' and then through the narrow channel of 's'. The uninterrupted breath can recall a cloth passing across a surface in one clean stroke.
usage_notes: Cleanliness comes from the quality-noun rule. Caused becoming gives 'hiso ka kelu' (clean), while 'sawa' (wash) names cleaning with water. 'shiloa' (pure) concerns composition, 'pesenu' (pollute) concerns impaired surroundings, and 'shurano' (safe) makes a risk judgement.

semantic_domains.physical: surfaces, objects, or materials free of unwanted matter for their present use
